# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — Current Codebase Version: 1.1 — Scope: branding, tokens, article content components, routing/import rules, accessibility, and safe one-shot code generation for the current React Router codebase. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form content that is visually consistent, inclusive, accessible, and compatible with this repository without guessing. Applies to: marketing pages, articles, programs/events pages, community resources, dashboards/apps, and any new long-form content. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette and code tokens MLAI’s current visual system combines modern tech teal tokens with bold editorial/brutalist article surfaces. The codebase uses Tailwind utilities, CSS variables, and article components that already encode most of the preferred styling. Use these tokens/classes where they already appear in the codebase: | Token / class | Role | |---|---| | `--brand` | Primary bright teal surface/accent. Used for bullets, accent borders, and focus-like emphasis. | | `--brand-ink` | Teal ink for links and hover states on light backgrounds. | | `--accent` | Charcoal/dark text on bright teal surfaces. | | `--soft` | Soft teal panel background. | | `#ff3d00` | Editorial orange surface used by article cards/callouts. | | `#4b1bd1` | Editorial purple surface used by article cards/callouts. | | `#ffe900` | Editorial yellow surface used by audience/event cards. | | `#f8f3e8` | Warm beige editorial panel, used by author bio surfaces. | | `bg-transparent` | Required neutral article wrapper/background class for new content wrappers. | #### Guardrails Normal text should remain WCAG-aware: - On light/transparent article backgrounds: - Use `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, or `text-black`. - Use `text-[--brand-ink]` for links and subtle brand emphasis. - Do not use low-contrast bright teal as body text. - On bright teal surfaces: - Use dark text such as `text-[--accent]` or `text-gray-900`. - Do not put body-sized white text on `--brand`. - On orange or purple editorial surfaces: - Use white text. - Prefer existing components such as `` and `` instead of recreating the styling. - On yellow editorial surfaces: - Use black text. - For wrappers: - Use `bg-transparent`. - Do **not** add `bg-white` to new article content wrappers. # Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is an Australian not-for-profit community for people building, learning and working with AI and machine learning. It runs events, meetups, hackathons, workshops, community programs, founder tools and related software/services to help Australia’s AI ecosystem grow through practical learning, real-world projects and meaningful professional connections.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Technology Community, Startup Ecosystem, Education/Events  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation; event organiser; educational/community program operator; software/founder tools provider  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia, with events and programs hosted across Australia; broader AI community impact “in Australia and beyond” where relevant

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun and impactful while empowering the Australian AI community. The organisation exists to foster a vibrant community of AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, professionals and students through meetups, hackathons, educational programs and practical collaboration.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To help make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs by building a thriving AI ecosystem through community, events, hackathons, founder support and educational programs.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, builders and professionals from diverse backgrounds.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world applications, working prototypes and useful outcomes rather than abstract AI hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise meaningful contributions through community systems such as Roo Points.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together developers, researchers, founders, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry partners.
- **Learning by doing:** Use hackathons, workshops, lightning talks and events to help people build skills through hands-on participation.
- **Ecosystem building:** Support the growth of Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup communities.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use this company as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI builders and practitioners:** Developers, data scientists, AI engineers and technical operators who want to learn, build projects, attend events, meet collaborators and apply AI to real-world problems.
- **Students and early-career learners:** People new to AI or machine learning who want accessible events, workshops, community support and practical ways to get involved.
- **Startup founders and AI entrepreneurs:** Founders building AI-enabled companies who need community, visibility, investor-network building, founder updates, mentoring and practical support before fundraising.
- **Researchers and technical professionals:** People working in AI, machine learning, data science or adjacent technical fields who want to connect with peers and contribute expertise.
- **Domain experts and professionals:** Clinicians, healthcare professionals, energy experts, safety professionals and others who can help shape practical AI solutions in specific sectors.
- **Sponsors, partners and ecosystem leaders:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel inaccessible, overhyped or difficult to apply in practical settings.
- Builders and learners often lack a supportive local community where they can meet collaborators and mentors.
- Founders may start raising money too late and need help building their investor network earlier.
- Professionals from non-technical domains may want to explore AI but need structured, collaborative environments.
- Hackathon participants need real problems, clear judging criteria, mentors and opportunities to prototype useful solutions.
- Volunteers and community contributors need transparent recognition for meaningful work.
- Sponsors and partners need credible ways to engage with Australia’s AI talent and startup ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, founders, researchers, developers and industry partners.
- Build prototypes and real-world AI/software projects through hackathons.
- Participate in events, meetups, workshops and lightning talks.
- Grow a startup network and prepare for future fundraising.
- Contribute to the community and earn recognition through Roo Points.
- Support or sponsor Australia’s AI ecosystem in a credible, useful way.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of the company’s offering as:

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   MLAI hosts events, meetups and workshops across Australia, including AI Week activities, startup-focused sessions and practical community gatherings. The events calendar is available at `/events`.

2. **Hackathons and challenge programs:**  
   MLAI runs or promotes AI/software hackathons focused on real-world problems. Examples from the website include:
   - **Watt The Hack:** Energy and AI hackathon for a cleaner, smarter energy future.
   - **MedHack: Frontiers:** Healthcare AI hackathon focused on medical innovation and real healthcare bottlenecks.
   - **eSafety Hackathon:** Online safety-focused innovation challenge.
   Hackathon information appears at `/hackathons` and specific pages such as `/medhack`.

3. **Community programs and Slack participation:**  
   MLAI operates community spaces including Slack/Discord-style collaboration channels. Members can participate, introduce themselves, support founders, share updates and volunteer.

4. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is the MLAI Slack helper for getting involved, checking points, booking coworking, viewing rewards and requesting community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions. They can be used for perks such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, merch and other Roo-managed rewards.

5. **Founder Tools / founder support:**  
   MLAI offers founder-oriented support that helps founders create updates, sharpen their signal and start building an investor network before raising. The website specifically says: “Most founders start raising when it’s too late.”

6. **Educational programs, workshops and resources:**  
   MLAI runs educational programs, workshops, talks, templates, guides and community learning materials for general educational and informational purposes.

7. **Software and project services:**  
   MLAI operates websites, founder tools, software, project services and connected-account products. Privacy documentation mentions products such as **Vibe Raising** for founder/investor updates and **Vibe Marketing** for AI-assisted marketing strategy and content workflows. Public-facing positioning for these products should be kept conservative unless a dedicated product page is provided.

8. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   MLAI offers sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs. The sponsorship page is `/sponsors`.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first AI ecosystem:** MLAI is positioned as a not-for-profit community, not just an event company or software vendor.
- **Australia-specific focus:** The organisation is explicitly focused on making Australia a strong home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs.
- **Practical, hands-on format:** Hackathons, workshops and events are framed around building real solutions to real problems.
- **Cross-disciplinary participation:** MLAI brings together technical builders, founders, researchers, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry professionals.
- **Contribution recognition system:** Roo Points provide an internal mechanism for recognising meaningful community contributions and converting them into practical perks.
- **Founder support embedded in community:** Founder Tools help startups create updates and build investor networks earlier, within a broader AI community context.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals (pick 1-2 per article, max):**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit organisation based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members from diverse backgrounds,” including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals and students.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks and large-scale hackathons.
- MLAI’s events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong demand for practical AI applications and professional connections.
- MLAI partners with organisations that share its vision for Australia’s AI future.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers is hosted in connection with Monash University Clayton Campus.
- MLAI provides community spaces, events, workshops, hackathons, founder tools, software, project services and educational programs.
- Roo lives inside the MLAI Slack workspace and helps manage community participation, rewards and requests.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do **not** claim MLAI guarantees jobs, funding, investment, introductions, prizes, rankings, commercial outcomes, technical outcomes, educational outcomes or business success.
- Do **not** present Roo Points as money, wages, a cash-value system, an exchange rate or employment compensation.
- Do **not** imply that earning Roo Points automatically guarantees an MLAI role, event ownership or paid work.
- Do **not** imply MLAI endorses every sponsor, partner, job listing, investment opportunity, community post or third-party offer unless explicitly stated.
- Do **not** present MLAI articles, events or hackathon content as legal, financial, tax, investment, medical, engineering, energy-market, environmental, policy, cybersecurity, accounting or other professional advice.
- Do **not** overclaim specific sponsor names, awards, certifications, funding raised, participant numbers for specific events or commercial results unless provided.
- Do **not** describe MLAI as a university, venture fund, accelerator or government body unless specifically supported by source content.
- Do **not** claim specific product capabilities for Vibe Raising or Vibe Marketing beyond what is described in the privacy policy unless a dedicated product page confirms them.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line (choose one per article)

- “MLAI is an Australian not-for-profit community helping builders, founders and learners apply AI through events, hackathons and practical programs.”
- “MLAI brings together Australia’s AI builders, researchers, students and entrepreneurs through hands-on events, hackathons and community support.”
- “MLAI is a community for people building and learning with AI in Australia.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools and community programs.”

### Primary CTAs (Use ONE per article, aligned to topic)

**For beginners/learners:**
> “New to AI or looking for a practical way in? Browse upcoming MLAI events and join a community of builders and learners.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Want to build with other AI practitioners? Explore upcoming MLAI hackathons and community events.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or `/events`

**For hackathon readers:**
> “Interested in solving real-world challenges with AI? Check the latest MLAI hackathon opportunities.”  
> Target: `/hackathons`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “If you’re interested in healthcare AI, explore MedHack: Frontiers and see how clinicians, builders and mentors work on real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “If you’re a founder, start building your investor network early with MLAI’s founder tools and community support.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` section mentioning Founder Tools, or [To be filled] if a dedicated Founder Tools URL exists

**For community contributors:**
> “Want to get more involved in the MLAI community? Meet Roo and learn how Roo Points recognise useful contributions.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Looking to connect with Australia’s AI builders, researchers and entrepreneurs? Explore MLAI’s events and partnership opportunities.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs building the future of AI.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### CTA Rules

- Use **ONE CTA per article**, ideally as a natural final line or short aside.
- Match the CTA to the article topic:
  - Learning/general AI → `/events`
  - Building/prototyping → `/hackathons`
  - Healthcare AI → `/medhack`
  - Community participation/rewards → `/roo`
  - Sponsorship/partnership → `/sponsors`
- Keep CTAs helpful and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- Avoid implying a guaranteed outcome from attending, joining, sponsoring or participating.
- Use MLAI’s own terminology: “events,” “hackathons,” “community,” “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “Roo Points,” “Slack,” “perks,” “practical AI applications.”
- When referencing Founder Tools, avoid inventing a URL unless confirmed. Use a generic CTA or mark the URL as [To be filled].

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use aggressive sales language such as “Buy now,” “Dominate AI,” “Get guaranteed investors,” or “Secure funding today.”
- Do not push Roo Points as a paid currency, job pathway or income opportunity.
- Do not promise mentorship, paid work, sponsorship, investor introductions or prizes unless specifically tied to an event page.
- Do not direct every reader to sponsor pages; sponsorship CTAs are only for partner/business-leader content.
- Do not position MLAI as providing professional advice, medical advice, fundraising advice or investment advice.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:** Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, optimistic and accessible. MLAI’s tone is energetic and inviting, but should stay grounded in real-world outcomes rather than hype.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over hype:** Focus on building, learning, prototyping and solving real problems.
- **Community-first:** Emphasise collaboration, contribution, participation and shared progress.
- **Accessible but credible:** Make AI feel approachable without oversimplifying technical or sector-specific issues.
- **Optimistic and action-oriented:** Encourage readers to get involved, meet people, build something and contribute.
- **Cross-disciplinary:** Welcome non-technical experts as well as technical builders.
- **Australian ecosystem-focused:** Frame MLAI as supporting Australia’s AI and machine learning community.

### Do’s

- Use inclusive language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “students,” “clinicians,” “researchers,” “mentors” and “community.”
- Use practical phrases from the site, such as:
  - “real-world challenges”
  - “practical AI applications”
  - “meaningful professional connections”
  - “help the community”
  - “get involved”
  - “build practical AI and software projects”
  - “real solutions to real bottlenecks”
- Use Australian English spelling where applicable: “organisation,” “recognise,” “programs” is acceptable because the site uses “programs,” “optimise” where relevant.
- Explain AI concepts in plain English before going deeper.
- Keep article advice educational and informational unless MLAI has explicitly agreed to provide professional services in that context.
- When writing about hackathons, emphasise teamwork, mentors, prototypes, judging criteria and real-world problem solving.
- When writing about Roo Points, include the internal-use caveat: they are recognition points, not money or wages.

### Don’ts

- Don’t use inflated AI hype such as “revolutionary,” “world-changing,” “unprecedented,” or “disrupt everything” unless quoting event copy directly.
- Don’t imply that AI alone solves complex healthcare, energy, safety or policy problems.
- Don’t make unsupported claims about event attendance, sponsor names, awards, university affiliations or participant outcomes.
- Don’t treat MLAI like a commercial SaaS company first; its primary identity is community/not-for-profit.
- Don’t overuse corporate jargon such as “synergise,” “unlock exponential value,” or “leverage AI transformation” without concrete meaning.
- Don’t write as if MLAI provides legal, financial, investment, medical or fundraising advice.
- Don’t frame community participation as transactional labour or employment.
- Don’t refer to Roo Points as cryptocurrency, tokens, cash, salary, wages or a marketplace currency.

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred.
- **Preferred terms:** MLAI, MLAI Aus, Australian AI community, AI and machine learning, builders, learners, founders, entrepreneurs, researchers, students, clinicians, practitioners, community, events, meetups, workshops, hackathons, Roo, Roo Points, Slack, perks, founder updates, investor network.
- **Avoided terms:** Guaranteed funding, guaranteed jobs, guaranteed investment, cash value, wage, salary, cryptocurrency, official endorsement, professional advice, medical advice, legal advice, investment advice, “AI will replace,” “AI solves everything.”

### Content Rules

- Present MLAI as an Australian not-for-profit AI and machine learning community, not primarily as a SaaS vendor, agency, accelerator or investment platform.
- Ground articles in practical value: what readers can learn, build, contribute or connect around.
- Use proof points sparingly. The strongest available statistic is “over 10,000 members,” but avoid repeating it in every article.
- If discussing MedHack, describe it as a healthcare AI hackathon bringing clinicians, builders and optimists together to prototype solutions to healthcare bottlenecks.
- If discussing Roo, always clarify that Roo Points are an internal thank-you/contribution recognition system and have no cash value.
- If discussing sponsors, position sponsorship as supporting Australia’s AI community and connecting with talented developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.
- If discussing founder support, focus on early investor-network building, founder updates and sharpening signal. Do not promise fundraising success.
- Do not imply MLAI guarantees outcomes from events, programs, hackathons, sponsorships or community participation.
- Avoid inventing program details, dates, prizes, sponsor names, mentor names or URLs not present in source content.
- Keep CTAs soft, relevant and community-aligned: invite readers to explore events, hackathons, Roo or sponsorship pages depending on the article topic.

# Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is an Australian not-for-profit community for people building, learning and working with AI and machine learning. It runs events, meetups, hackathons, workshops, community programs, founder tools and related software/services to help Australia’s AI ecosystem grow through practical learning, real-world projects and meaningful professional connections.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Technology Community, Startup Ecosystem, Education/Events  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation; event organiser; educational/community program operator; software/founder tools provider  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia, with events and programs hosted across Australia; broader AI community impact “in Australia and beyond” where relevant

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun and impactful while empowering the Australian AI community. The organisation exists to foster a vibrant community of AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, professionals and students through meetups, hackathons, educational programs and practical collaboration.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To help make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs by building a thriving AI ecosystem through community, events, hackathons, founder support and educational programs.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, builders and professionals from diverse backgrounds.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world applications, working prototypes and useful outcomes rather than abstract AI hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise meaningful contributions through community systems such as Roo Points.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together developers, researchers, founders, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry partners.
- **Learning by doing:** Use hackathons, workshops, lightning talks and events to help people build skills through hands-on participation.
- **Ecosystem building:** Support the growth of Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup communities.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use this company as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI builders and practitioners:** Developers, data scientists, AI engineers and technical operators who want to learn, build projects, attend events, meet collaborators and apply AI to real-world problems.
- **Students and early-career learners:** People new to AI or machine learning who want accessible events, workshops, community support and practical ways to get involved.
- **Startup founders and AI entrepreneurs:** Founders building AI-enabled companies who need community, visibility, investor-network building, founder updates, mentoring and practical support before fundraising.
- **Researchers and technical professionals:** People working in AI, machine learning, data science or adjacent technical fields who want to connect with peers and contribute expertise.
- **Domain experts and professionals:** Clinicians, healthcare professionals, energy experts, safety professionals and others who can help shape practical AI solutions in specific sectors.
- **Sponsors, partners and ecosystem leaders:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel inaccessible, overhyped or difficult to apply in practical settings.
- Builders and learners often lack a supportive local community where they can meet collaborators and mentors.
- Founders may start raising money too late and need help building their investor network earlier.
- Professionals from non-technical domains may want to explore AI but need structured, collaborative environments.
- Hackathon participants need real problems, clear judging criteria, mentors and opportunities to prototype useful solutions.
- Volunteers and community contributors need transparent recognition for meaningful work.
- Sponsors and partners need credible ways to engage with Australia’s AI talent and startup ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, founders, researchers, developers and industry partners.
- Build prototypes and real-world AI/software projects through hackathons.
- Participate in events, meetups, workshops and lightning talks.
- Grow a startup network and prepare for future fundraising.
- Contribute to the community and earn recognition through Roo Points.
- Support or sponsor Australia’s AI ecosystem in a credible, useful way.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of the company’s offering as:

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   MLAI hosts events, meetups and workshops across Australia, including AI Week activities, startup-focused sessions and practical community gatherings. The events calendar is available at `/events`.

2. **Hackathons and challenge programs:**  
   MLAI runs or promotes AI/software hackathons focused on real-world problems. Examples from the website include:
   - **Watt The Hack:** Energy and AI hackathon for a cleaner, smarter energy future.
   - **MedHack: Frontiers:** Healthcare AI hackathon focused on medical innovation and real healthcare bottlenecks.
   - **eSafety Hackathon:** Online safety-focused innovation challenge.
   Hackathon information appears at `/hackathons` and specific pages such as `/medhack`.

3. **Community programs and Slack participation:**  
   MLAI operates community spaces including Slack/Discord-style collaboration channels. Members can participate, introduce themselves, support founders, share updates and volunteer.

4. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is the MLAI Slack helper for getting involved, checking points, booking coworking, viewing rewards and requesting community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions. They can be used for perks such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, merch and other Roo-managed rewards.

5. **Founder Tools / founder support:**  
   MLAI offers founder-oriented support that helps founders create updates, sharpen their signal and start building an investor network before raising. The website specifically says: “Most founders start raising when it’s too late.”

6. **Educational programs, workshops and resources:**  
   MLAI runs educational programs, workshops, talks, templates, guides and community learning materials for general educational and informational purposes.

7. **Software and project services:**  
   MLAI operates websites, founder tools, software, project services and connected-account products. Privacy documentation mentions products such as **Vibe Raising** for founder/investor updates and **Vibe Marketing** for AI-assisted marketing strategy and content workflows. Public-facing positioning for these products should be kept conservative unless a dedicated product page is provided.

8. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   MLAI offers sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs. The sponsorship page is `/sponsors`.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first AI ecosystem:** MLAI is positioned as a not-for-profit community, not just an event company or software vendor.
- **Australia-specific focus:** The organisation is explicitly focused on making Australia a strong home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs.
- **Practical, hands-on format:** Hackathons, workshops and events are framed around building real solutions to real problems.
- **Cross-disciplinary participation:** MLAI brings together technical builders, founders, researchers, clinicians, students, mentors, sponsors and industry professionals.
- **Contribution recognition system:** Roo Points provide an internal mechanism for recognising meaningful community contributions and converting them into practical perks.
- **Founder support embedded in community:** Founder Tools help startups create updates and build investor networks earlier, within a broader AI community context.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals (pick 1-2 per article, max):**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit organisation based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members from diverse backgrounds,” including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals and students.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks and large-scale hackathons.
- MLAI’s events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong demand for practical AI applications and professional connections.
- MLAI partners with organisations that share its vision for Australia’s AI future.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers is hosted in connection with Monash University Clayton Campus.
- MLAI provides community spaces, events, workshops, hackathons, founder tools, software, project services and educational programs.
- Roo lives inside the MLAI Slack workspace and helps manage community participation, rewards and requests.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do **not** claim MLAI guarantees jobs, funding, investment, introductions, prizes, rankings, commercial outcomes, technical outcomes, educational outcomes or business success.
- Do **not** present Roo Points as money, wages, a cash-value system, an exchange rate or employment compensation.
- Do **not** imply that earning Roo Points automatically guarantees an MLAI role, event ownership or paid work.
- Do **not** imply MLAI endorses every sponsor, partner, job listing, investment opportunity, community post or third-party offer unless explicitly stated.
- Do **not** present MLAI articles, events or hackathon content as legal, financial, tax, investment, medical, engineering, energy-market, environmental, policy, cybersecurity, accounting or other professional advice.
- Do **not** overclaim specific sponsor names, awards, certifications, funding raised, participant numbers for specific events or commercial results unless provided.
- Do **not** describe MLAI as a university, venture fund, accelerator or government body unless specifically supported by source content.
- Do **not** claim specific product capabilities for Vibe Raising or Vibe Marketing beyond what is described in the privacy policy unless a dedicated product page confirms them.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line (choose one per article)

- “MLAI is an Australian not-for-profit community helping builders, founders and learners apply AI through events, hackathons and practical programs.”
- “MLAI brings together Australia’s AI builders, researchers, students and entrepreneurs through hands-on events, hackathons and community support.”
- “MLAI is a community for people building and learning with AI in Australia.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools and community programs.”

### Primary CTAs (Use ONE per article, aligned to topic)

**For beginners/learners:**
> “New to AI or looking for a practical way in? Browse upcoming MLAI events and join a community of builders and learners.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Want to build with other AI practitioners? Explore upcoming MLAI hackathons and community events.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or `/events`

**For hackathon readers:**
> “Interested in solving real-world challenges with AI? Check the latest MLAI hackathon opportunities.”  
> Target: `/hackathons`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “If you’re interested in healthcare AI, explore MedHack: Frontiers and see how clinicians, builders and mentors work on real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “If you’re a founder, start building your investor network early with MLAI’s founder tools and community support.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` section mentioning Founder Tools, or [To be filled] if a dedicated Founder Tools URL exists

**For community contributors:**
> “Want to get more involved in the MLAI community? Meet Roo and learn how Roo Points recognise useful contributions.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Looking to connect with Australia’s AI builders, researchers and entrepreneurs? Explore MLAI’s events and partnership opportunities.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers and entrepreneurs building the future of AI.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### CTA Rules

- Use **ONE CTA per article**, ideally as a natural final line or short aside.
- Match the CTA to the article topic:
  - Learning/general AI → `/events`
  - Building/prototyping → `/hackathons`
  - Healthcare AI → `/medhack`
  - Community participation/rewards → `/roo`
  - Sponsorship/partnership → `/sponsors`
- Keep CTAs helpful and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- Avoid implying a guaranteed outcome from attending, joining, sponsoring or participating.
- Use MLAI’s own terminology: “events,” “hackathons,” “community,” “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “Roo Points,” “Slack,” “perks,” “practical AI applications.”
- When referencing Founder Tools, avoid inventing a URL unless confirmed. Use a generic CTA or mark the URL as [To be filled].

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use aggressive sales language such as “Buy now,” “Dominate AI,” “Get guaranteed investors,” or “Secure funding today.”
- Do not push Roo Points as a paid currency, job pathway or income opportunity.
- Do not promise mentorship, paid work, sponsorship, investor introductions or prizes unless specifically tied to an event page.
- Do not direct every reader to sponsor pages; sponsorship CTAs are only for partner/business-leader content.
- Do not position MLAI as providing professional advice, medical advice, fundraising advice or investment advice.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:** Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, optimistic and accessible. MLAI’s tone is energetic and inviting, but should stay grounded in real-world outcomes rather than hype.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over hype:** Focus on building, learning, prototyping and solving real problems.
- **Community-first:** Emphasise collaboration, contribution, participation and shared progress.
- **Accessible but credible:** Make AI feel approachable without oversimplifying technical or sector-specific issues.
- **Optimistic and action-oriented:** Encourage readers to get involved, meet people, build something and contribute.
- **Cross-disciplinary:** Welcome non-technical experts as well as technical builders.
- **Australian ecosystem-focused:** Frame MLAI as supporting Australia’s AI and machine learning community.

### Do’s

- Use inclusive language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “students,” “clinicians,” “researchers,” “mentors” and “community.”
- Use practical phrases from the site, such as:
  - “real-world challenges”
  - “practical AI applications”
  - “meaningful professional connections”
  - “help the community”
  - “get involved”
  - “build practical AI and software projects”
  - “real solutions to real bottlenecks”
- Use Australian English spelling where applicable: “organisation,” “recognise,” “programs” is acceptable because the site uses “programs,” “optimise” where relevant.
- Explain AI concepts in plain English before going deeper.
- Keep article advice educational and informational unless MLAI has explicitly agreed to provide professional services in that context.
- When writing about hackathons, emphasise teamwork, mentors, prototypes, judging criteria and real-world problem solving.
- When writing about Roo Points, include the internal-use caveat: they are recognition points, not money or wages.

### Don’ts

- Don’t use inflated AI hype such as “revolutionary,” “world-changing,” “unprecedented,” or “disrupt everything” unless quoting event copy directly.
- Don’t imply that AI alone solves complex healthcare, energy, safety or policy problems.
- Don’t make unsupported claims about event attendance, sponsor names, awards, university affiliations or participant outcomes.
- Don’t treat MLAI like a commercial SaaS company first; its primary identity is community/not-for-profit.
- Don’t overuse corporate jargon such as “synergise,” “unlock exponential value,” or “leverage AI transformation” without concrete meaning.
- Don’t write as if MLAI provides legal, financial, investment, medical or fundraising advice.
- Don’t frame community participation as transactional labour or employment.
- Don’t refer to Roo Points as cryptocurrency, tokens, cash, salary, wages or a marketplace currency.

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred.
- **Preferred terms:** MLAI, MLAI Aus, Australian AI community, AI and machine learning, builders, learners, founders, entrepreneurs, researchers, students, clinicians, practitioners, community, events, meetups, workshops, hackathons, Roo, Roo Points, Slack, perks, founder updates, investor network.
- **Avoided terms:** Guaranteed funding, guaranteed jobs, guaranteed investment, cash value, wage, salary, cryptocurrency, official endorsement, professional advice, medical advice, legal advice, investment advice, “AI will replace,” “AI solves everything.”

### Content Rules

- Present MLAI as an Australian not-for-profit AI and machine learning community, not primarily as a SaaS vendor, agency, accelerator or investment platform.
- Ground articles in practical value: what readers can learn, build, contribute or connect around.
- Use proof points sparingly. The strongest available statistic is “over 10,000 members,” but avoid repeating it in every article.
- If discussing MedHack, describe it as a healthcare AI hackathon bringing clinicians, builders and optimists together to prototype solutions to healthcare bottlenecks.
- If discussing Roo, always clarify that Roo Points are an internal thank-you/contribution recognition system and have no cash value.
- If discussing sponsors, position sponsorship as supporting Australia’s AI community and connecting with talented developers, researchers and entrepreneurs.
- If discussing founder support, focus on early investor-network building, founder updates and sharpening signal. Do not promise fundraising success.
- Do not imply MLAI guarantees outcomes from events, programs, hackathons, sponsorships or community participation.
- Avoid inventing program details, dates, prizes, sponsor names, mentor names or URLs not present in source content.
- Keep CTAs soft, relevant and community-aligned: invite readers to explore events, hackathons, Roo or sponsorship pages depending on the article topic.

spec.title: How Do AI Detectors Work, and When Should You Trust Them?
spec.meta_description: How do AI detectors work? Learn what they measure, why scores can be wrong, and how to use results responsibly.

summary_items.label: How do AI detectors even detect AI?
summary_items.description: AI detectors analyse writing patterns such as word choice, sentence structure, predictability and style. Many compare those signals with examples of human-written and AI-generated text, then return a likelihood score.

summary_items.label: How to trick AI content detectors?
summary_items.description: Trying to trick a detector is not a reliable or responsible approach. The better response is transparent authorship, clear drafting records, and human review when a result is disputed.

summary_items.label: How to 100% humanize AI text?
summary_items.description: There is no guaranteed way to make AI text pass every detector, and detector scores can still vary. Focus on honest use, careful editing, accurate sources and clear disclosure where required.

summary_question_candidates.question: How do AI detectors even detect AI?
How to trick AI content detectors?

faq_items.question: Is 40% AI detection bad?
faq_items.answer: Not automatically. A score such as 40% depends on the tool, threshold, text length, genre and review context. It should be treated as a signal for further review, not a verdict.

faq_items.question: Do AI detector scores prove cheating or misconduct?
faq_items.answer: No. AI detectors estimate whether text resembles patterns linked with AI-generated writing. They do not prove who wrote the text or how it was created.

faq_items.question: Why do different AI detectors give different results?
faq_items.answer: Different tools use different training data, thresholds, models and scoring methods. Results can also change with language, text length, genre and whether the writing was edited or mixed with human input.

faq_items.question: What should you do if your work is falsely flagged?
faq_items.answer: Gather context such as drafts, notes, version history, source material, assignment instructions and an explanation of your writing process. Ask for a human review rather than relying on the score alone.

faq_items.question: When should organisations use AI detectors?
faq_items.answer: They are best used as screening tools within a clear review process. Policies should explain which tools are used, what flags mean, who reviews results and how people can respond.

sections.heading: AI detectors estimate patterns, not truth
paragraphs: AI detectors work by analysing text for patterns often linked with machine-generated writing. They look at signals such as word choice, sentence structure, writing style and statistical predictability, then estimate how likely it is that AI was involved.
A detector score is a probability signal, not proof of authorship or misconduct.

sections.heading: The signals AI detectors look for
paragraphs: AI detectors do not read a piece of writing the way a person does. They analyse the text for patterns that often appear in AI-generated writing. A detector may also look at whether the vocabulary and structure match examples it has seen before from human-written and AI-generated text.

AI-generated text can sometimes look more uniform than human writing, because each word and sentence follows a likely pattern. Human writing is often messier. But these are only clues. Polished human writing can also be consistent, and edited AI-assisted writing can look less predictable.

Many detectors also use model-based classification. This means a machine learning model is trained on examples of human and AI-written text, then learns patterns that may separate the two groups. That estimate is not proof. It is a probability based on patterns in the text.

sections.heading: How the detection pipeline works
paragraphs: An AI detector starts by taking the submitted text and breaking it into smaller pieces it can analyse. Those pieces may be sentences, words, tokens, or passages. This makes the writing easier to compare against patterns the tool has learned from human-written and AI-generated text.

The detector then looks for signals in the writing. Common signals include word choice, sentence structure, predictability, burstiness, and repeated patterns that often appear in generated text. A scoring system or classifier combines those signals and returns an estimate, such as a likelihood, label, or percentage.

subsections.heading: Why a percentage is not proof
paragraphs: A detector score is an estimate, not a finding of fact. The tool is judging patterns in the text, not proving who wrote it. A high percentage means the writing looks similar to text the detector associates with AI generation. It does not prove that an AI system wrote it.
The score can help start a closer look at the writing, but it should not be used on its own to make a serious decision about a student, worker, author, or applicant.

sections.heading: Why AI detectors can be wrong
paragraphs: AI detectors do not prove authorship. They estimate whether a piece of text looks more like known AI-generated writing or known human writing. A formal report, a polished essay, or a templated workplace document can sometimes look predictable enough to be flagged, even when a person wrote it.

The two main failure modes are false positives and false negatives. A false positive happens when human writing is marked as AI-generated. This can matter in education, publishing, hiring, legal work, and government reporting because a wrong flag can damage trust or lead to unfair action. A false negative happens when AI-generated text is missed, especially if it has been edited, rewritten, or mixed with human input.

Results can also vary from one detector to another. Performance may change with text length, language, genre, dataset, and the type of AI system that produced the writing. Newer AI models can also make detection harder because detectors are often comparing current text against patterns learned from earlier examples.

For high-stakes decisions, an AI detector should be treated as one signal, not the final answer. It can support a review process, but it should not replace context, human judgment, version history, citations, interviews, or other evidence about how the work was created.

sections.heading: What AI detector scores mean in practice
paragraphs: An AI detector score is an estimate, not a verdict. A result like “40% AI” is not automatically good or bad. Its meaning depends on the detector, the threshold it uses, the length and type of text, and the setting in which the text is being reviewed.

In simple terms, AI detectors look for patterns in writing. They analyse style, structure, word use, sentence patterns, and other linguistic or statistical features. Many tools compare those signals with examples of human-written and AI-generated text, then return a likelihood score. That score can help start a review, but it should not replace human judgement.

This is why two tools can disagree on the same text. A detector can also be wrong. If a piece of work is flagged unfairly, the better response is to gather context: drafts, notes, version history, assignment instructions, source material, and a clear explanation of the writing process.

subsections.heading: Common score questions
paragraphs: How do scores work? They usually express the tool’s estimate that the text shares patterns with AI-generated writing. They do not show exactly what happened during writing.
Do scores prove cheating? No.
Why do tools disagree?
What should you do if work is falsely flagged? Instead, be transparent about any AI use, keep drafting records, and ask for a human review of the evidence.

sections.heading: A responsible way to use AI detectors
paragraphs: Use an AI detector as a screening tool, not as a final decision-maker. The strongest use is to treat a detector result as one signal alongside the document itself, the context of the task, the person’s usual writing, source quality, citations, and any available drafts or version history. This matters in education, publishing, workplaces, and community settings because AI use is now common, while detector results still have limits.

A fair review process should move in stages. First, run the check and note what the tool has flagged. If the concern remains, ask the writer for context, such as how they researched, drafted, edited, or used AI support.

People should know which tools may be used, what a flag does and does not mean, who reviews the result, and how they can respond. For MLAI’s audience, this is part of broader AI literacy: detection can support governance and integrity, but it becomes risky when treated as an automated judge.
bullets: Run the detector and record the result.
Inspect the flagged passages in context.

sections.heading: Use detectors to start better reviews
paragraphs: AI detectors work by estimating whether a piece of text looks like known AI-generated writing. They analyse style, structure, wording, and patterns, then return a likelihood or score. That makes them useful as a signal when AI use is a real question, but not reliable as a final verdict on their own.

The better practice is to treat a detector result as the start of a review, not the end of one. Understand the tool's limits before using it. Set clear and transparent policies before scores are used in education, publishing, hiring, or any other high-stakes setting.

Use detectors as one way to learn how AI systems behave, where they fail, and how people can use them responsibly. Good AI literacy means asking better questions, not looking for a shortcut around careful review.

cta.title: Build better AI literacy
cta.body: Learn how AI tools work, where they fail, and how to use them with clearer judgement in study, work and community settings.

notes: Use the grounded sections as the article body. The article should frame AI detection as probabilistic review support, not proof. Include the required featured-article shell components, at least one step-list presentation for the responsible workflow, references, disclaimer, author bio and footer navigation. Do not add unsupported claims about detector accuracy rates.
Selected summary questions: How do AI detectors even detect AI? (paa, vol=0, ai=0); How to trick AI content detectors? (paa, vol=0, ai=1); How to 100% humanize AI text? (paa, vol=0, ai=76)

claim_bindings.claim_text: Do scores prove cheating?
No.
A score can raise a question, but it does not prove misconduct or authorship on its own.
It should be reviewed alongside the task, the writer’s process, and any available evidence.
Why do tools disagree?
They may analyse different features and apply different thresholds.
Some focus more on sentence structure, others on statistical regularity or comparison with known examples.

package.title: How Do AI Detectors Work, and When Should You Trust Them?
package.meta_description: How do AI detectors work? Learn what they measure, why scores can be wrong, and how to use results responsibly.
package.article_markdown:
# How Do AI Detectors Work, and When Should You Trust Them?

How do AI detectors work? Learn what they measure, why scores can be wrong, and how to use results responsibly.

![Close-up of coworkers reviewing AI detector scores on a laptop during a candid discussion](https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Fhero-172f9074-80ba-4343-b788-edc24b810e7b.jpg?alt=media&token=8ec5c2ea-3709-48a3-b592-171a52abbbc2)
*How do AI detectors work? Learn what they measure, why scores can be wrong, and how to use results responsibly.*

## Quick Summary

- **How do AI detectors even detect AI?:** AI detectors analyse writing patterns such as word choice, sentence structure, predictability and style. Many compare those signals with examples of human-written and AI-generated text, then return a likelihood score.
- **How to trick AI content detectors?:** Trying to trick a detector is not a reliable or responsible approach. The better response is transparent authorship, clear drafting records, and human review when a result is disputed.
- **How to 100% humanize AI text?:** There is no guaranteed way to make AI text pass every detector, and detector scores can still vary. Focus on honest use, careful editing, accurate sources and clear disclosure where required.

## AI detectors estimate patterns, not truth

AI detectors work by analysing text for patterns often linked with machine-generated writing. They look at signals such as word choice, sentence structure, writing style and statistical predictability, then estimate how likely it is that AI was involved.

A detector score is a probability signal, not proof of authorship or misconduct.

## The signals AI detectors look for

AI detectors do not read a piece of writing the way a person does. They analyse the text for patterns that often appear in AI-generated writing. A detector may also look at whether the vocabulary and structure match examples it has seen before from human-written and AI-generated text.

AI-generated text can sometimes look more uniform than human writing, because each word and sentence follows a likely pattern. Human writing is often messier. But these are only clues. Polished human writing can also be consistent, and edited AI-assisted writing can look less predictable.

Many detectors also use model-based classification. This means a machine learning model is trained on examples of human and AI-written text, then learns patterns that may separate the two groups. That estimate is not proof. It is a probability based on patterns in the text.

![Laptop text analysis dashboard beside marked notes and coffee on a cluttered desk](https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-681655a3-b770-4e09-8de6-dce295dafe41.jpg?alt=media&token=bab57be1-00a6-4542-b1f3-7324dcd2045b)
*The signals AI detectors look for*

## How the detection pipeline works

An AI detector starts by taking the submitted text and breaking it into smaller pieces it can analyse. Those pieces may be sentences, words, tokens, or passages. This makes the writing easier to compare against patterns the tool has learned from human-written and AI-generated text.

The detector then looks for signals in the writing. Common signals include word choice, sentence structure, predictability, burstiness, and repeated patterns that often appear in generated text. A scoring system or classifier combines those signals and returns an estimate, such as a likelihood, label, or percentage.

## Why AI detectors can be wrong

AI detectors do not prove authorship. They estimate whether a piece of text looks more like known AI-generated writing or known human writing. A formal report, a polished essay, or a templated workplace document can sometimes look predictable enough to be flagged, even when a person wrote it.

The two main failure modes are false positives and false negatives. A false positive happens when human writing is marked as AI-generated. This can matter in education, publishing, hiring, legal work, and government reporting because a wrong flag can damage trust or lead to unfair action. A false negative happens when AI-generated text is missed, especially if it has been edited, rewritten, or mixed with human input.

Results can also vary from one detector to another. Performance may change with text length, language, genre, dataset, and the type of AI system that produced the writing. Newer AI models can also make detection harder because detectors are often comparing current text against patterns learned from earlier examples.

For high-stakes decisions, an AI detector should be treated as one signal, not the final answer. It can support a review process, but it should not replace context, human judgment, version history, citations, interviews, or other evidence about how the work was created.

![Laptop on a quiet desk showing an AI writing report beside notes in a candid workspace setting](https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-c9d02c8d-c6eb-46a8-a7bc-48b9dec108d4.jpg?alt=media&token=a02491d7-475a-49b3-93c9-cac30ca7f4d9)
*Why AI detectors can be wrong*

## What AI detector scores mean in practice

An AI detector score is an estimate, not a verdict. A result like “40% AI” is not automatically good or bad. Its meaning depends on the detector, the threshold it uses, the length and type of text, and the setting in which the text is being reviewed.

In simple terms, AI detectors look for patterns in writing. They analyse style, structure, word use, sentence patterns, and other linguistic or statistical features. Many tools compare those signals with examples of human-written and AI-generated text, then return a likelihood score. That score can help start a review, but it should not replace human judgement.

This is why two tools can disagree on the same text. A detector can also be wrong. If a piece of work is flagged unfairly, the better response is to gather context: drafts, notes, version history, assignment instructions, source material, and a clear explanation of the writing process.

![Close-up of hands reviewing an AI detector score on a laptop in a candid workspace](https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-d6f8f8f3-1405-4b0a-838c-43dbe01fac5a.jpg?alt=media&token=cced79cc-24e8-4153-8ff1-db41a23ec7f3)
*What AI detector scores mean in practice*

## A responsible way to use AI detectors

Use an AI detector as a screening tool, not as a final decision-maker. The strongest use is to treat a detector result as one signal alongside the document itself, the context of the task, the person’s usual writing, source quality, citations, and any available drafts or version history. This matters in education, publishing, workplaces, and community settings because AI use is now common, while detector results still have limits.

A fair review process should move in stages. First, run the check and note what the tool has flagged. If the concern remains, ask the writer for context, such as how they researched, drafted, edited, or used AI support.

People should know which tools may be used, what a flag does and does not mean, who reviews the result, and how they can respond. For MLAI’s audience, this is part of broader AI literacy: detection can support governance and integrity, but it becomes risky when treated as an automated judge.

- Run the detector and record the result.
- Inspect the flagged passages in context.

## Use detectors to start better reviews

AI detectors work by estimating whether a piece of text looks like known AI-generated writing. They analyse style, structure, wording, and patterns, then return a likelihood or score. That makes them useful as a signal when AI use is a real question, but not reliable as a final verdict on their own.

The better practice is to treat a detector result as the start of a review, not the end of one. Understand the tool's limits before using it. Set clear and transparent policies before scores are used in education, publishing, hiring, or any other high-stakes setting.

Use detectors as one way to learn how AI systems behave, where they fail, and how people can use them responsibly. Good AI literacy means asking better questions, not looking for a shortcut around careful review.

![Review team discussing AI detector results on a laptop in a candid office meeting](https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-b6687d14-5cef-43e0-bd1f-8599263ee17c.jpg?alt=media&token=97827a0b-cf65-4ea6-8f03-b6f73239c395)
*Use detectors to start better reviews*

## FAQ

### Is 40% AI detection bad?

Not automatically. A score such as 40% depends on the tool, threshold, text length, genre and review context. It should be treated as a signal for further review, not a verdict.

### Do AI detector scores prove cheating or misconduct?

No. AI detectors estimate whether text resembles patterns linked with AI-generated writing. They do not prove who wrote the text or how it was created.

### Why do different AI detectors give different results?

Different tools use different training data, thresholds, models and scoring methods. Results can also change with language, text length, genre and whether the writing was edited or mixed with human input.

### What should you do if your work is falsely flagged?

Gather context such as drafts, notes, version history, source material, assignment instructions and an explanation of your writing process. Ask for a human review rather than relying on the score alone.

### When should organisations use AI detectors?

They are best used as screening tools within a clear review process. Policies should explain which tools are used, what flags mean, who reviews results and how people can respond.

## Next Step

**Build better AI literacy**

Learn how AI tools work, where they fail, and how to use them with clearer judgement in study, work and community settings.
